title: Flight path shake-up plan for 'outdated' 1950s airspace over Scotland

sections.heading: What happened
sections.content: The plans are designed to reduce noise and cut emissions during take offs and landings Edinburgh and Glasgow airports could see the biggest shake-up of Scotland's airspace since the 1950s under new flight path proposals. A public consultation has opened into the plans, which are designed to reduce noise and cut emissions during take offs and landings. The Airspace Change Organising Group says modern aircraft are still using "outdated routes" from 70 years ago - often flying further and longer than needed.

sections.heading: Key details
sections.content: The Scottish Airspace Modernisation consultation is jointly co-ordinated between Edinburgh Airport, Glasgow Airport and air traffic service Nats. The design of the UK's airspace is largely based on a network of ground navigation beacons which when introduced in the 1950s guided an average 200,000 flights annually in the UK. Air travel has mushroomed in the decades since and by 2019 there was a yearly average of 2.5 million flights with projections of 3 million by 2030.

sections.heading: Why it matters
sections.content: Aircraft often fly further than necessary at sub-optimal altitudes and speeds because the routes were originally intended to fly over the location of the beacons instead of following shorter, more efficient flight paths. Nats said the revised routes could bring a reduction of 18,000 tonnes of CO2e each year – approximately the emissions from the energy used by 5,000 homes.

sourceReferences.citationText: serper-scottish-aviation – Flight path shake-up plan for 'outdated' 1950s airspace over Scotland
